# policy write - Command | Vault | HashiCorp Developer

[HashiConf The full conference agenda's now LIVE. Buy your pass and plan your schedule. Register](https://www.hashicorp.com/en/conferences/hashiconf/register)

Dismiss alert

[Vault Home](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault)

policy write
============

The `policy write` command uploads a policy with name NAME from the contents of a local file PATH or stdin. If PATH is "-", the policy is read from stdin. Otherwise, it is loaded from the file at the given path on the local disk.

For details on the policy syntax, please see the [policy documentation](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/concepts/policies)
.

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/policy/write#examples)
Examples
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Upload a policy named "my-policy" from "/tmp/policy.hcl" on the local disk:

    $ vault policy write my-policy /tmp/policy.hcl
    

Upload a policy from stdin:

    $ cat my-policy.hcl | vault policy write my-policy -
    

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/policy/write#usage)
Usage
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

There are no flags beyond the [standard set of flags](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands)
 included on all commands.

[Edit this page on GitHub](https://github.com/hashicorp/web-unified-docs/blob/main/content/vault/v1.20.x/content/docs/commands/policy/write.mdx)

---

# pki reissue - Command | Vault | HashiCorp Developer

[HashiConf The full conference agenda's now LIVE. Buy your pass and plan your schedule. Register](https://www.hashicorp.com/en/conferences/hashiconf/register)

Dismiss alert

[Vault Home](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault)

pki reissue
===========

Frequently, a reissued CA certificate is to be very similar to another. This command enables reissuing a CA, using an existing issuer within Vault as a template, but allowing modifications to the desired attributes.

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#usage)
Usage
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Usage: `vault pki reissue [flags] <parent> <template> <child_mount> [options]`

*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#)
    
    [`[flags]`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#flags)
     are optional arguments described below.
    
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#)
    
    [`<parent>`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#)
     is the fully qualified path of the Certificate Authority in vault which will issue the new intermediate certificate.
    
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#)
    
    [`<template>`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#-1)
     is the fully qualified path of an intermediate certificate in vault which will be used to populate certificate fields not overridden by `[options]`.
    

**Note**: not all possible certificate fields are supported by Vault, and this template reader covers only those vault generates as a best effort. If unknown fields are set, such as when an external CA was imported into Vault, there may not be a warning that those are missing from the new issuer.

*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#)
    
    [`<child_mount>`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#-2)
     is the path of the mount in vault where the new issuer is saved.
    
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#)
    
    [`[options]`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#options)
     are the superset of the k=v options passed to generate/intermediate and sign-intermediate commands. See [below](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#options)
    .
    

The output of this command when it is successful is to read the resulting new issuer entry.

### [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#flags-1)
Flags

*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#)
    [`-type`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#type)
     `(string: "internal")` - This determines the type of key use for the newly created certificate. Valid types are `"existing"` - where we link to a key already present in the vault-backend to be used - `"internal"` - to generate a new key for this certificate - or `"kms"` - to link to an external key. Exported keys are not available through this API.

**Note**: It is only possible to generate a new certificate with an existing key that exists in the same mount where that key-material exists. This command is expected to fail should the template exist on a different mount, `existing` is the selected type, and no `key_ref` for a key in the new issuer mount is provided.

*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#)
    [`-issuer_name`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#issuer_name)
     `(string: "")` - If present, the newly created issuer will be given this name.

### [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#options-1)
Options

Other than `type` (which is passed as a flag, see above), this command accepts all options provided to the [Generate CSR](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/api-docs/secret/pki#generate-intermediate-csr)
 and [Sign Intermediate](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/api-docs/secret/pki#sign-intermediate)
 endpoints.

### [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#accessed-apis)
Accessed APIs

Note that the vault user running this command will need to have access to the following API endpoints, each representing a step in the process:

*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#)
    [`READ /:parent`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#read-parent)
     - used to check validity
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#)
    [`READ /:template`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#read-template)
     - used to generate the options for the new certificate
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#)
    [`WRITE /:child_mount/intermediate/generate/:type`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#write-child_mount-intermediate-generate-type)
     - used to generate the csr
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#)
    [`WRITE /:parent/sign-intermediate`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#write-parent-sign-intermediate)
     - used to sign the csr
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#)
    [`WRITE /:child_mount/issuers/import/cert`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#write-child_mount-issuers-import-cert)
     - used to import the new issuer, and the issuer chain
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#)
    [`UPDATE /:child_mount/issuer/:issuer_refs`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#update-child_mount-issuer-issuer_refs)
     - used to both name the new issuer, and also set the name of the parent in the issuer chain
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#)
    [`READ /:child_mount/issuer/:new_issuer_ref`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#read-child_mount-issuer-new_issuer_ref)
     - used to verify completion, generate the output

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/pki/reissue#examples)
Examples
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

    $ vault pki reissue -issuer_name="SecondDepartment" /pki_root/issuer/default /pki_int/issuer/FirstDepartment /pki_int_2/ common_name="second-department.example.com"
    Key                               Value
    ---                               -----
    ca_chain                          [-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----\
    MIID0DCCArigAwIBAgIUdfRe05B5eRXsg3pvsJ/g94eYuWkwDQYJKoZIhvcNAQEL```\
    \
    \
\
[Edit this page on GitHub](https://github.com/hashicorp/web-unified-docs/blob/main/content/vault/v1.20.x/content/docs/commands/pki/reissue.mdx)

---

# UI - Configuration | Vault | HashiCorp Developer

[HashiConf The full conference agenda's now LIVE. Buy your pass and plan your schedule. Register](https://www.hashicorp.com/en/conferences/hashiconf/register)

Dismiss alert

[Vault Home](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault)

You are viewing documentation for version v1.9.x. [View latest version](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/configuration/ui)
.

Vault UI
========

Vault features a user interface (web interface) for interacting with Vault. Easily create, read, update, and delete secrets, authenticate, unseal, and more with the Vault UI.

The UI requires **Vault 0.10 or higher** or Vault Enterprise.

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.9.x/configuration/ui#activating-the-vault-ui)
Activating the Vault UI
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Vault UI is not activated by default. To activate the UI, set the `ui` configuration option in the Vault server configuration. Vault clients do not need to set this option, since they will not be serving the UI.

    ui = true
    
    listener "tcp" {
      # ...
    }
    

For more information, please see the [Vault configuration options](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/configuration)
.

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.9.x/configuration/ui#accessing-the-vault-ui)
Accessing the Vault UI
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The UI runs on the same port as the Vault listener. As such, you must configure at least one `listener` stanza in order to access the UI.

    listener "tcp" {
      address = "10.0.1.35:8200"
    
      # If bound to localhost, the Vault UI is only
      # accessible from the local machine!
      # address = "127.0.0.1:8200"
    }
    

In this case, the UI is accessible at the following URL from any machine on the subnet (provided no network firewalls are in place):

    https://10.0.1.35:8200/ui/
    

It is also accessible at any DNS entry that resolves to that IP address, such as the Consul service address (if using Consul):

    https://vault.service.consul:8200/ui/
    

### [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.9.x/configuration/ui#note-on-tls)
Note on TLS

When using TLS (recommended), the certificate must be valid for all DNS entries you will be accessing the Vault UI on, and any IP addresses on the SAN. If you are running Vault with a self-signed certificate, any browsers that access the Vault UI will need to have the root CA installed. Failure to do so may result in the browser displaying a warning that the site is "untrusted". It is highly recommended that client browsers accessing the Vault UI install the proper CA root for validation to reduce the chance of a MITM attack.

[Edit this page on GitHub](https://github.com/hashicorp/web-unified-docs/blob/main/content/vault/v1.9.x/content/docs/configuration/ui.mdx)

---

# Vault Enterprise Redundancy Zones | Vault | HashiCorp Developer

[HashiConf The full conference agenda's now LIVE. Buy your pass and plan your schedule. Register](https://www.hashicorp.com/en/conferences/hashiconf/register)

Dismiss alert

[Vault Home](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault)

You are viewing documentation for version v1.13.x. [View latest version](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/enterprise/redundancy-zones)
.

Redundancy zones
================

**Note**: This feature requires [Vault Enterprise](https://www.hashicorp.com/products/vault/)
 configured to use Integrated Storage.

Vault Enterprise Redundancy Zones provide both read scaling and resiliency benefits by enabling the deployment of non-voting nodes alongside voting nodes on a per availability zone basis.

When using redundancy zones, if an operator chooses to deploy Vault across three availability zones, they could have two (or more) nodes (one voting/one+ non-voting) in each zone. In the event that a voting node in an availability zone fails, the redundancy zone configuration automatically promotes the non-voting node to a voting node. In the event that an entire availability zone is lost, a non-voting node in one of the existing availability zones would be promoted to a voting node, keeping quorum. This capability functions as a "hot standby" for server nodes while also providing enhanced read scalability.

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.13.x/enterprise/redundancy-zones#configuration)
Configuration
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

A new key can be added to Vault's `storage` configuration stanza: `autopilot_redundancy_zone`. The value for this key is a string of your choosing and represents the zone this particular node should be in.

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.13.x/enterprise/redundancy-zones#mechanics)
Mechanics
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Vault's Autopilot subsystem will always attempt to maintain exactly one voting node per redundancy zone. Any additional nodes beyond the first one will be demoted to non-voting status. Non-voting nodes can serve reads but can not participate in cluster elections.

If redundancy zones are used in conjunction with automated upgrades, Autopilot will always try to ensure that Vault is never moving from a more healthy state to a less healthy state. Autopilot will wait to begin leadership transfer until it can ensure that there will be as much redundancy on the new Vault version as there was on the old Vault version.

The status of redundancy zones can be monitored by consulting the [Autopilot state API endpoint](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/api-docs/system/storage/raftautopilot#get-cluster-state)
.

[Edit this page on GitHub](https://github.com/hashicorp/web-unified-docs/blob/main/content/vault/v1.13.x/content/docs/enterprise/redundancy-zones.mdx)

---

# Log Completed Requests - Configuration | Vault | HashiCorp Developer

[HashiConf The full conference agenda's now LIVE. Buy your pass and plan your schedule. Register](https://www.hashicorp.com/en/conferences/hashiconf/register)

Dismiss alert

[Vault Home](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault)

You are viewing documentation for version v1.16.x. [View latest version](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/configuration/log-requests-level)
.

Log completed requests
======================

Vault can be configured to log completed requests using the `log_requests_level` configuration parameter.

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.16.x/configuration/log-requests-level#activating-logging-completed-requests)
Activating logging completed requests
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

By default, logging completed requests is disabled. To activate request logging, set the `log_requests_level` configuration option in the Vault server configuration to the desired logging level. The acceptable logging levels are `error`, `warn`, `info`, `debug`, `trace`, and `off`, which is the default.

Completed requests will be logged at the configured level, if Vault's log level includes this level of logs. For example, if `log_level` is set to `debug`, but `log_requests_level` is set to `trace`, completed requests will not be logged.

If the Vault server is already running, you can still configure the parameter in the Vault server configuration, and then send a `SIGHUP` signal to the Vault process.

    log_requests_level = "debug"
    log_level = "debug"
    
    listener "tcp" {
      # ...
    }
    

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.16.x/configuration/log-requests-level#deactivating-logging-completed-requests)
Deactivating logging completed requests
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

To deactivate logging completed requests, simply remove the `log_requests_level` configuration parameter from the Vault server configuration, or set it to `off`, and send a `SIGHUP` signal to the Vault process.

    log_requests_level = "off"
    log_level = "debug"
    
    listener "tcp" {
      # ...
    }
    

[Edit this page on GitHub](https://github.com/hashicorp/web-unified-docs/blob/main/content/vault/v1.16.x/content/docs/configuration/log-requests-level.mdx)

---

# Managed Keys | Vault | HashiCorp Developer

[HashiConf The full conference agenda's now LIVE. Buy your pass and plan your schedule. Register](https://www.hashicorp.com/en/conferences/hashiconf/register)

Dismiss alert

[Vault Home](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault)

You are viewing documentation for version v1.16.x. [View latest version](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/enterprise/managed-keys)
.

Managed keys
============

EnterpriseEnterprise

Appropriate [Vault Enterprise](https://www.hashicorp.com/products/vault/pricing)
 license or [HCP Vault Dedicated](https://developer.hashicorp.com/hcp/docs/vault/tiers-and-features)
 cluster required.

Within certain environments, customers want to leverage key management systems external to Vault, when handling, storing, and interacting with private key material, or are required to do so by standards requirements.

To satisfy these requirements, Vault has a centralized abstraction called _Managed Keys_ that different secrets engines can plug into, allowing them to delegate these operations to a trusted external KMS.

Minimally, a managed key consists of a named managed key entry managed by the [`sys/managed-key`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/api-docs/system/managed-keys)
 API. Besides a name, there are backend specific configurations to access the key in question.

For PKCS#11 (HSM) backed managed keys, the managed key configuration must reference a [kms library stanza](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/configuration/kms-library)
 which points to a PKCS#11 access library on the host machine.

Note that a configured, named managed key corresponds to a single key within a backend. More than one managed key can be configured targeting a single backend by creating multiple managed keys with the API.

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.16.x/enterprise/managed-keys#namespace-support)
Namespace support
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Every configured Managed Key is bound to a given namespace, defaulting to the root namespace. Any secrets engine's mount path must exist within the same namespace as the Managed Key for which it intends to use.

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.16.x/enterprise/managed-keys#backend-support)
Backend support
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Managed Keys were developed to support different types of external backends. At this time supported backends are PKCS#11, AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault, and Google Cloud KMS. Support for additional integrations may be added in the future.

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.16.x/enterprise/managed-keys#secret-and-auth-engine-support)
Secret and auth engine support
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The [PKI Secrets Engine](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/api-docs/secret/pki#managed-keys)
 has been integrated with Managed Keys to offer certificate generation, both root and intermediary PKI paths, leveraging private keys from an external trusted KMS.

The [Transit Secrets Engine](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/api-docs/secret/transit#managed-keys)
 allows users to generate and verify cryptographic signatures with Managed Keys, and in some cases encrypt and decrypt data, or generate MACs and HMACs. These operations are performed on the KMS or HSM that hosts the Managed Key material.

More engines may leverage managed keys in the future.

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.16.x/enterprise/managed-keys#api)
API
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Managed Keys can be managed over the HTTP API. Please see [Managed Keys API](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/api-docs/system/managed-keys)
 for more details.

To configure PKI secrets engine with Managed Keys please see [PKI Secret API](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/api-docs/secret/pki#managed-keys)

[Edit this page on GitHub](https://github.com/hashicorp/web-unified-docs/blob/main/content/vault/v1.16.x/content/docs/enterprise/managed-keys.mdx)

---

# delete - Command | Vault | HashiCorp Developer

[HashiConf The full conference agenda's now LIVE. Buy your pass and plan your schedule. Register](https://www.hashicorp.com/en/conferences/hashiconf/register)

Dismiss alert

[Vault Home](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault)

You are viewing documentation for version v1.7.x. [View latest version](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/delete)
.

delete
======

The `delete` command deletes secrets and configuration from Vault at the given path. The behavior of "delete" is delegated to the backend corresponding to the given path.

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.7.x/commands/delete#examples)
Examples
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Remove data in the static secrets engine:

    $ vault delete secret/my-secret
    

Uninstall an encryption key in the transit backend:

    $ vault delete transit/keys/my-key
    

Delete an IAM role:

    $ vault delete aws/roles/ops
    

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.7.x/commands/delete#usage)
Usage
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------

There are no flags beyond the [standard set of flags](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands)
 included on all commands.

[Edit this page on GitHub](https://github.com/hashicorp/web-unified-docs/blob/main/content/vault/v1.7.x/content/docs/commands/delete.mdx)

# Plugin runtime settings | Vault | HashiCorp Developer

[HashiConf The full conference agenda's now LIVE. Buy your pass and plan your schedule. Register](https://www.hashicorp.com/en/conferences/hashiconf/register)

Dismiss alert

[Vault Home](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault)

You are viewing documentation for version v1.19.x. [View latest version](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/plugins/plugin-management)
.

Plugin runtime settings
=======================

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.19.x/plugins/plugin-management#plugin-environment-variables)
Plugin environment variables
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

An advantage for external plugins over builtin plugins is they can specify additional environment variables because they are run in their own process.

Vault 1.16.0 changed the precedence given to plugin-specific environment variables so they take priority over Vault's environment. See full details in the [upgrade notes](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/upgrading/upgrade-to-1.16.x)
.

Use the `-env` flag once per environment variable that a plugin should be started with:

    $ vault plugin register -sha256=<SHA256 Hex value of the plugin binary> \
        -env REGION=eu \
        -env TOKEN_FILE=/var/run/token \
        secret \                  # type
        passthrough-plugin
    
    Success! Registered plugin: passthrough-plugin
    

### [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.19.x/plugins/plugin-management#plugin-specific-http-proxy-settings)
Plugin-specific HTTP proxy settings

Many tools and libraries automatically consume `HTTP_PROXY`, `HTTPS_PROXY`, and `NO_PROXY` environment variables to configure HTTP proxy settings, including the Go standard library's default HTTP client. You can use these environment variables to configure different network proxies for different plugins:

You must be using an external plugin to take advantage of custom environment variables. If you are using a builtin plugin, you can still download and register an external version of it in order to use this workflow. Check the [HashiCorp releases page](https://releases.hashicorp.com/)
 for the latest prebuilt plugin binaries.

#### [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.19.x/plugins/plugin-management#community-plugins)
Community plugins

    $ vault plugin register -sha256=<SHA256 Hex value of the plugin binary> \
        -env HTTP_PROXY=eu.example.com \
        auth \
        jwt-eu
    
    Success! Registered plugin: jwt-eu
    
    $ vault plugin register -sha256=<SHA256 Hex value of the plugin binary> \
        -env HTTP_PROXY=us.example.com \
        auth \
        jwt-us
    
    Success! Registered plugin: jwt-us
    

#### [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.19.x/plugins/plugin-management#enterprise-plugins)
Enterprise plugins

    $ vault plugin register -version=<version of the plugin> \
        -env HTTP_PROXY=example.com \
        secret \
        vault-plugin-secrets-keymgmt
    
    Success! Registered plugin: vault-plugin-secrets-keymgmt
    

You can then enable each plugin on its own path, and configure clients that should be associated with one or the other appropriately:

    $ vault auth enable jwt-eu
    Success! Enabled the jwt-eu auth method at: auth/jwt-eu/
    
    $ vault auth enable jwt-us
    Success! Enabled the jwt-us auth method at: auth/jwt-us/
    
    $ vault secrets enable vault-plugin-secrets-keymgmt
    Success! Enabled the vault-plugin-secrets-keymgmt secrets engine at: vault-plugin-secrets-keymgmt/
    

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.19.x/plugins/plugin-management#troubleshooting)
Troubleshooting
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.19.x/plugins/plugin-management#unrecognized-remote-plugin-message)
Unrecognized remote plugin message

If the following error is encountered when enabling a plugin secret engine or auth method:

    Unrecognized remote plugin message:
    
    This usually means that the plugin is either invalid or simply
    needs to be recompiled to support the latest protocol.
    

Verify whether the Vault process has `mlock` enabled, and if so, run the following command against the plugin binary:

    $ sudo setcap cap_ipc_lock=+ep <plugin-binary>
    

[Edit this page on GitHub](https://github.com/hashicorp/web-unified-docs/blob/main/content/vault/v1.19.x/content/docs/plugins/plugin-management.mdx)

---

# Prevent lease explosions | Vault | HashiCorp Developer

[HashiConf The full conference agenda's now LIVE. Buy your pass and plan your schedule. Register](https://www.hashicorp.com/en/conferences/hashiconf/register)

Dismiss alert

[Vault Home](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault)

You are viewing documentation for version v1.18.x. [View latest version](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/configuration/prevent-lease-explosions)
.

Prevent lease explosions
========================

As your Vault environment scales to meet deployment needs, you run the risk of lease explosions. Lease explosions can occur when a Vault cluster is over-subscribed and clients overwhelm system resources with consistent, high-volume API requests

Unchecked lease explosions create a memory drain on the active node, which can cascade to other nodes and result in denial-of-service issues for the entire cluster.

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.18.x/configuration/prevent-lease-explosions#look-for-early-warning-signs)
Look for early warning signs
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Cleaning up after a lease explosion is time consuming and resource intensive, so we strongly recommend monitoring your Vault instance for signals that your Vault deployment has matured and requires tuning:

| Issue | Possible cause |
| --- | --- |
| Unused leases consume storage space for extended periods while waiting to expire | The TTL values for dynamic secret leases or authentication tokens may be too high |
| Lease revocation fails frequently | Failures in an external service (e.g., for dynamic secrets) |
| Build up of leases associated with unused credentials | Clients are not reusing valid, existing leases |
| Lease revocation is slow | Insufficient IOPS for the storage backend |
| Rapid lease count growth disproportionate to the number of clients | Misconfiguration or anti-patterns in client usage |

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.18.x/configuration/prevent-lease-explosions#enforce-client-best-practices)
Enforce client best practices
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

High lease counts can degrade system performance:

*   Use the smallest default time-to-live (TTL) possible for tokens and leases to avoid excessive unexpired lease backlogs and high-volume, simultaneous expirations.
*   Review telemetry for aberrant client behavior that might lead to rapid over-subscription.
*   Limit the number of simultaneous dynamic secret requests and service token authentication requests.
*   Ensure that machine clients adhere to [recommended AppRole patterns](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/tutorials/recommended-patterns/pattern-approle)
    .
*   Review [AppRole best practices](https://www.hashicorp.com/blog/how-and-why-to-use-approle-correctly-in-hashicorp-vault)
    .

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.18.x/configuration/prevent-lease-explosions#set-reasonable-ttl-guardrails)
Set reasonable TTL guardrails
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Choose appropriate defaults for your situation and use resource quotas as guardrails against lease explosion. You can set default and maximum TTLs globally, in the mount configuration for a specific authN or secrets plugin, and at the role-level (e.g., database credential roles).

Vault prioritizes TTL values by granularity:

*   Global values act as the default.
*   Plugin TTL values override global values.
*   Role, group, and user level TTL values override plugin and global values.

TTL changes are not retroactive

Leases and tokens keep the TTL value in affect during their creation. When you adjust TTL values, the new limits only apply to leases and tokens issued after you deploy the changes.

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.18.x/configuration/prevent-lease-explosions#monitor-key-metrics-and-logs)
Monitor key metrics and logs
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Proactive monitoring is key to finding problematic behavior and usage patterns before they escalate:

*   Review [key Vault metrics](https://developer.hashicorp.com/well-architected-framework/reliability/reliability-vault-monitoring-key-metrics)
    
*   Understand [metric anti-patterns](https://developer.hashicorp.com/well-architected-framework/operational-excellence/security-vault-anti-patterns#poor-metrics-or-no-telemetry-data)
    
*   Monitor [Vault audit device logs](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/tutorials/monitoring/monitor-telemetry-audit-splunk)
     for quota-related failures.

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.18.x/configuration/prevent-lease-explosions#control-resource-usage-with-quotas)
Control resource usage with quotas
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Use API rate limiting quotas and [lease count quotas](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/tutorials/operations/resource-quotas#lease-count-quotas)
 to limit the number of leases generated on a per-mount basis and control resource consumption for your Vault instance where hard limits makes sense.

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.18.x/configuration/prevent-lease-explosions#consider-batch-tokens)
Consider batch tokens
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If your environment inherently leads to a large number of lease requests, consider using batch tokens over service tokens.

The following resources can help you decide if batch tokens are reasonable for your situation:

*   [Vault service tokens vs batch tokens](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/tutorials/tokens/batch-tokens#service-tokens-vs-batch-tokens)
    
*   [Service vs batch token lease handling](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/concepts/tokens#service-vs-batch-token-lease-handling)
    

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.18.x/configuration/prevent-lease-explosions#next-steps)
Next steps
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Proactive monitoring and periodic usage analysis can help you identify potential problems before they escalate.

*   Brush up on [general Vault resource quotas](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/concepts/resource-quotas)
     in general.
*   Learn about [lease count quotas for Vault Enterprise](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/enterprise/lease-count-quotas)
    .
*   Learn how to [query audit device logs](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/tutorials/monitoring/query-audit-device-logs)
    .
*   Review [recommended Vault lease limits](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/internals/limits#lease-limits)
    .
*   Review [lease anti-patterns](https://developer.hashicorp.com/well-architected-framework/operational-excellence/security-vault-anti-patterns#not-adjusting-the-default-lease-time)
     for a clear explanation of the issue and solution.

[Edit this page on GitHub](https://github.com/hashicorp/web-unified-docs/blob/main/content/vault/v1.18.x/content/docs/configuration/prevent-lease-explosions.mdx)

---

# Telemetry - Configuration | Vault | HashiCorp Developer

[HashiConf The full conference agenda's now LIVE. Buy your pass and plan your schedule. Register](https://www.hashicorp.com/en/conferences/hashiconf/register)

Dismiss alert

[Vault Home](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault)

You are viewing documentation for version v1.14.x. [View latest version](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/configuration/telemetry)
.

telemetry stanza
================

The `telemetry` stanza specifies various configurations for Vault to publish metrics to upstream systems. Available Vault metrics can be found in the [Telemetry internals documentation](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/internals/telemetry)
.

    telemetry {
      statsite_address = "statsite.company.local:8125"
    }
    

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#telemetry-parameters)
`telemetry` parameters
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Due to the number of configurable parameters to the `telemetry` stanza, parameters on this page are grouped by the telemetry provider.

### [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#common)
Common

The following options are available on all telemetry configurations.

*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#)
    [`usage_gauge_period`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#usage_gauge_period)
     `(string: "10m")` - Specifies the interval at which high-cardinality usage data is collected, such as token counts, entity counts, and secret counts. A value of "none" disables the collection. Uses [duration format strings](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/concepts/duration-format)
    .
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#)
    [`maximum_gauge_cardinality`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#maximum_gauge_cardinality)
     `(int: 500)` - The maximum cardinality of gauge labels.
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#)
    [`disable_hostname`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#disable_hostname)
     `(bool: false)` - Specifies if gauge values should be prefixed with the local hostname.
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#)
    [`enable_hostname_label`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#enable_hostname_label)
     `(bool: false)` - Specifies if all metric values should contain the `host` label with the local hostname. It is recommended to enable `disable_hostname` if this option is used.
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#)
    [`lease_metrics_epsilon`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#lease_metrics_epsilon)
     `(string: "1h")` - Specifies the size of the bucket used to measure future lease expiration. For example, for the default value of 1 hour, the `vault.expire.leases.by_expiration` metric will aggregate the total number of expiring leases for 1 hour buckets, starting from the current time. Note that leases are put into buckets by rounding. For example, if `lease_metrics_epsilon` is set to 1h and lease A expires 25 minutes from now, and lease B expires 35 minutes from now, then lease A will be in the first bucket, which corresponds to 0-30 minutes, and lease B will be in the second bucket, which corresponds to 31-90 minutes. Uses [duration format strings](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/concepts/duration-format)
    .
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#)
    [`num_lease_metrics_buckets`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#num_lease_metrics_buckets)
     `(int: 168)` - The number of expiry buckets for leases. For the default value, for example, 168 value labels for the `vault.expire.leases.by_expiration` metric will be reported, where each value each bucket is separated in time by the `lease_metrics_epsilon` parameter. For the default 1 hour value of `lease_metrics_epsilon` and the default value of `num_lease_metrics_buckets`, `vault.expire.leases.by_expiration` will report the total number of leases expiring within each hour from the current time to one week from the current time.
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#)
    [`add_lease_metrics_namespace_labels`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#add_lease_metrics_namespace_labels)
     `(bool: false)` - If this value is set to true, then `vault.expire.leases.by_expiration` will break down expiring leases by both time and namespace. This parameter is disabled by default because enabling it can lead to a large-cardinality metric.
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#)
    [`filter_default`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#filter_default)
     `(bool: true)` - This controls whether to allow metrics that have not been specified by the filter. Defaults to `true`, which will allow all metrics when no filters are provided. When set to `false` with no filters, no metrics will be sent.
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#)
    [`prefix_filter`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#prefix_filter)
     `(string array: [])` - This is a list of filter rules to apply for allowing/blocking metrics by prefix in the following format:
    
        ["+vault.token", "-vault.expire", "+vault.expire.num_leases"]
        
    
    A leading "**+**" will enable any metrics with the given prefix, and a leading "**\-**" will block them. If there is overlap between two rules, the more specific rule will take precedence. Blocking will take priority if the same prefix is listed multiple times.

### [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#statsite)
`statsite`

These `telemetry` parameters apply to [statsite](https://github.com/armon/statsite)
.

*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#)
    [`statsite_address`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#statsite_address)
     `(string: "")` - Specifies the address of a statsite server to forward metrics data to.

    telemetry {
      statsite_address = "statsite.company.local:8125"
    }
    

### [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#statsd)
`statsd`

These `telemetry` parameters apply to [statsd](https://github.com/etsy/statsd)
.

*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#)
    [`statsd_address`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#statsd_address)
     `(string: "")` - Specifies the address of a statsd server to forward metrics to.

    telemetry {
      statsd_address = "statsd.company.local:8125"
    }
    

### [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#circonus)
`circonus`

These `telemetry` parameters apply to [Circonus](http://circonus.com/)
.

*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#)
    
    [`circonus_api_token`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#circonus_api_token)
     `(string: "")` - Specifies a valid Circonus API Token used to create/manage check. If provided, metric management is enabled.
    
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#)
    
    [`circonus_api_app`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#circonus_api_app)
     `(string: "nomad")` - Specifies a valid app name associated with the API token.
    
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#)
    
    [`circonus_api_url`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#circonus_api_url)
     `(string: "https://api.circonus.com/v2")` - Specifies the base URL to use for contacting the Circonus API.
    
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#)
    
    [`circonus_submission_interval`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#circonus_submission_interval)
     `(string: "10s")` - Specifies the interval at which metrics are submitted to Circonus.
    
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#)
    
    [`circonus_submission_url`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#circonus_submission_url)
     `(string: "")` - Specifies the `check.config.submission_url` field, of a Check API object, from a previously created HTTPTRAP check.
    
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#)
    
    [`circonus_check_id`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#circonus_check_id)
     `(string: "")` - Specifies the Check ID (**not check bundle**) from a previously created HTTPTRAP check. The numeric portion of the `check._cid` field in the Check API object.
    
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#)
    
    [`circonus_check_force_metric_activation`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#circonus_check_force_metric_activation)
     `(bool: false)` - Specifies if force activation of metrics which already exist and are not currently active. If check management is enabled, the default behavior is to add new metrics as they are encountered. If the metric already exists in the check, it will not be activated. This setting overrides that behavior.
    
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#)
    
    [`circonus_check_instance_id`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#circonus_check_instance_id)
     `(string: "<hostname>:<application>")` - Serves to uniquely identify the metrics coming from this _instance_. It can be used to maintain metric continuity with transient or ephemeral instances as they move around within an infrastructure. By default, this is set to hostname:application name (e.g. "host123:nomad").
    
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#)
    
    [`circonus_check_search_tag`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#circonus_check_search_tag)
     `(string: <service>:<application>)` - Specifies a special tag which, when coupled with the instance id, helps to narrow down the search results when neither a Submission URL or Check ID is provided. By default, this is set to service:app (e.g. "service:nomad").
    
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#)
    
    [`circonus_check_display_name`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#circonus_check_display_name)
     `(string: "")` - Specifies a name to give a check when it is created. This name is displayed in the Circonus UI Checks list.
    
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#)
    
    [`circonus_check_tags`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#circonus_check_tags)
     `(string: "")` - Comma separated list of additional tags to add to a check when it is created.
    
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#)
    
    [`circonus_broker_id`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#circonus_broker_id)
     `(string: "")` - Specifies the ID of a specific Circonus Broker to use when creating a new check. The numeric portion of `broker._cid` field in a Broker API object. If metric management is enabled and neither a Submission URL nor Check ID is provided, an attempt will be made to search for an existing check using Instance ID and Search Tag. If one is not found, a new HTTPTRAP check will be created. By default, this is a random Enterprise Broker is selected, or, the default Circonus Public Broker.
    
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#)
    
    [`circonus_broker_select_tag`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#circonus_broker_select_tag)
     `(string: "")` - Specifies a special tag which will be used to select a Circonus Broker when a Broker ID is not provided. The best use of this is to as a hint for which broker should be used based on _where_ this particular instance is running (e.g. a specific geo location or datacenter, dc:sfo).
    

### [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#dogstatsd)
`dogstatsd`

These `telemetry` parameters apply to [DogStatsD](http://docs.datadoghq.com/guides/dogstatsd/)
.

*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#)
    [`dogstatsd_addr`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#dogstatsd_addr)
     `(string: "")` - This provides the address of a DogStatsD instance. DogStatsD is a protocol-compatible flavor of statsd, with the added ability to decorate metrics with tags and event information. If provided, Vault will send various telemetry information to that instance for aggregation. This can be used to capture runtime information.

*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#)
    [`dogstatsd_tags`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#dogstatsd_tags)
     `(string array: [])` - This provides a list of global tags that will be added to all telemetry packets sent to DogStatsD. It is a list of strings, where each string looks like "my\_tag\_name:my\_tag\_value".

### [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#prometheus)
`prometheus`

These `telemetry` parameters apply to [prometheus](https://prometheus.io/)
.

*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#)
    [`prometheus_retention_time`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#prometheus_retention_time)
     `(string: "24h")` - Specifies the amount of time that Prometheus metrics are retained in memory. Setting this to 0 will disable Prometheus telemetry.
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#)
    [`disable_hostname`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#disable_hostname-1)
     `(bool: false)` - It is recommended to also enable the option `disable_hostname` to avoid having prefixed metrics with hostname.

The `/v1/sys/metrics` endpoint is only accessible on active nodes and automatically disabled on standby nodes. You can enable the `/v1/sys/metrics` endpoint on standby nodes by [enabling unauthenticated metrics access](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/configuration/listener/tcp#telemetry-parameters)
.

Vault does not use the default Prometheus path, so Prometheus must be configured with the path below. Note that using `?format=prometheus` in the path won't work as "?" will be escaped, so it must be specified as a parameter.

A Vault token is required with `capabilities = ["read", "list"]` to /v1/sys/metrics. The Prometheus `bearer_token` or `bearer_token_file` options must be added to the scrape job.

An example job\_name stanza required in the [Prometheus config](https://prometheus.io/docs/prometheus/latest/configuration/configuration/#scrape_config)
 is provided below.

    # prometheus.yml
    scrape_configs:
      - job_name: 'vault'
        metrics_path: "/v1/sys/metrics"
        params:
          format: ['prometheus']
        scheme: https
        tls_config:
          ca_file: your_ca_here.pem
        bearer_token: "your_vault_token_here"
        static_configs:
        - targets: ['your_vault_server_here:8200']
    

An example telemetry configuration to be added to Vault's configuration file is shown below:

    telemetry {
      prometheus_retention_time = "30s"
      disable_hostname = true
    }
    

### [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#stackdriver)
`stackdriver`

These `telemetry` parameters apply to [Stackdriver Monitoring](https://cloud.google.com/monitoring/)
.

The Stackdriver telemetry provider uses the official Google Cloud Golang SDK. This means it supports the common ways of [providing credentials to Google Cloud](https://cloud.google.com/docs/authentication/production#providing_credentials_to_your_application)
.

To use this telemetry provider, the service account must have the following minimum scope(s):

    https://www.googleapis.com/auth/cloud-platform
    https://www.googleapis.com/auth/monitoring
    https://www.googleapis.com/auth/monitoring.write
    

And the following IAM role(s):

    roles/monitoring.metricWriter
    

*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#)
    [`stackdriver_project_id`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#stackdriver_project_id)
     `(string: "")` - The Google Cloud ProjectID to send telemetry data to.
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#)
    [`stackdriver_location`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#stackdriver_location)
     `(string: "")` - The GCP or AWS region of the monitored resource.
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#)
    [`stackdriver_namespace`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#stackdriver_namespace)
     `(string: "")` - A namespace identifier for the telemetry data.
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#)
    [`stackdriver_debug_logs`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.14.x/configuration/telemetry#stackdriver_debug_logs)
     `(bool: "false")` - Specifies if Vault writes additional stackdriver related debug logs to standard error output (stderr).

It is recommended to also enable the option `disable_hostname` to avoid having prefixed metrics with hostname and enable instead `enable_hostname_label`.

    telemetry {
      stackdriver_project_id = "my-test-project"
      stackdriver_location = "us-east1-a"
      stackdriver_namespace = "vault-cluster-a"
      disable_hostname = true
      enable_hostname_label = true
    }
    

Metrics from Vault can be found in [Metrics Explorer](https://cloud.google.com/monitoring/charts/metrics-explorer)
. All those metrics are shown with a resource type of `generic_task`, and the metric name is prefixed with `custom.googleapis.com/go-metrics/`.

[Edit this page on GitHub](https://github.com/hashicorp/web-unified-docs/blob/main/content/vault/v1.14.x/content/docs/configuration/telemetry.mdx)

---

# Entropy Augmentation - Configuration | Vault | HashiCorp Developer

[HashiConf The full conference agenda's now LIVE. Buy your pass and plan your schedule. Register](https://www.hashicorp.com/en/conferences/hashiconf/register)

Dismiss alert

[Vault Home](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault)

You are viewing documentation for version v1.16.x. [View latest version](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/configuration/entropy-augmentation)
.

Entropy augmentation seal
=========================

Entropy augmentation enables Vault to sample entropy from external cryptographic modules. Sourcing external entropy is done by configuring a supported [Seal](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/configuration/seal)
 type which include: [PKCS11 seal](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/configuration/seal/pkcs11)
, [AWS KMS](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/configuration/seal/awskms)
, and [Vault Transit](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/configuration/seal/transit)
. Vault Enterprises's external entropy support is activated by the presence of an `entropy "seal"` block in Vault's configuration file.

**Note**: If using the Seal High Availability Beta, entropy will be retrieved from seals in priority order, using bytes from the first available and online seal.

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.16.x/configuration/entropy-augmentation#requirements)
Requirements
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

A valid Vault Enterprise license is required for Entropy Augmentation.

**Warning** This feature is not available with FIPS 140-2 Inside variants of Vault.

Additionally, the following software packages and enterprise modules are required for sourcing entropy via the [PKCS11 seal](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/configuration/seal/pkcs11)
:

*   Vault Enterprise with the Plus package
*   PKCS#11 compatible HSM integration library. Vault targets version 2.2 or higher of PKCS#11. Depending on any given HSM, some functions (such as key generation) may have to be performed manually.
*   The [GNU libltdl library](https://www.gnu.org/software/libtool/manual/html_node/Using-libltdl)
     — ensure that it is installed for the correct architecture of your servers

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.16.x/configuration/entropy-augmentation#entropy-example)
`entropy` example
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This example shows configuring entropy augmentation through a PKCS11 HSM seal from Vault's configuration file:

    seal "pkcs11" {
        ...
    }
    
    entropy "seal" {
        mode = "augmentation"
    }
    

For a more detailed tutorial, visit the [HSM Entropy Challenge](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/tutorials/enterprise/hsm-entropy)
 on HashiCorp's Learn website.

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.16.x/configuration/entropy-augmentation#entropy-augmentation-parameters)
`entropy augmentation` parameters
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

These parameters apply to the `entropy` stanza in the Vault configuration file:

*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.16.x/configuration/entropy-augmentation#)
    [`mode`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.16.x/configuration/entropy-augmentation#mode)
     `(string: <required>)`: The mode determines which Vault operations requiring entropy will sample entropy from the external source. Currently, the only mode supported is `augmentation` which sources entropy for [Critical Security Parameters (CSPs)](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/enterprise/entropy-augmentation#critical-security-parameters-csps)
    .

[Edit this page on GitHub](https://github.com/hashicorp/web-unified-docs/blob/main/content/vault/v1.16.x/content/docs/configuration/entropy-augmentation.mdx)

---

# delete - Command | Vault | HashiCorp Developer

[HashiConf The full conference agenda's now LIVE. Buy your pass and plan your schedule. Register](https://www.hashicorp.com/en/conferences/hashiconf/register)

Dismiss alert

[Vault Home](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault)

You are viewing documentation for version v1.15.x. [View latest version](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/delete)
.

delete
======

The `delete` command deletes secrets and configuration from Vault at the given path (wrapper command for HTTP DELETE). The behavior of "delete" is delegated to the backend corresponding to the given path.

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.15.x/commands/delete#examples)
Examples
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Remove data in the static secrets engine:

    $ vault delete secret/my-secret
    

Uninstall an encryption key in the transit backend:

    $ vault delete transit/keys/my-key
    

Note: changing the `deletion_allowed` parameter to `true` is necessary for the key to be successfully deleted, you can read more on key parameters [here](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/api-docs/secret/transit#update-key-configuration)

Delete an IAM role:

    $ vault delete aws/roles/ops
    

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.15.x/commands/delete#usage)
Usage
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

There are no flags beyond the [standard set of flags](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands)
 included on all commands.

[Edit this page on GitHub](https://github.com/hashicorp/web-unified-docs/blob/main/content/vault/v1.15.x/content/docs/commands/delete.mdx)

---

# Plugin Development — Event Notifications | Vault | HashiCorp Developer

[HashiConf The full conference agenda's now LIVE. Buy your pass and plan your schedule. Register](https://www.hashicorp.com/en/conferences/hashiconf/register)

Dismiss alert

[Vault Home](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault)

You are viewing documentation for version v1.18.x. [View latest version](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/plugins/plugin-development-event-notifications)
.

Event notifications development in Vault plugins
================================================

EnterpriseEnterprise

Appropriate [Vault Enterprise](https://www.hashicorp.com/products/vault/pricing)
  license required

Advanced topic! Plugin development is a highly advanced topic in Vault, and is not required knowledge for day-to-day usage. If you don't plan on writing any plugins, we recommend not reading this section of the documentation.

[Event notifications](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/concepts/events)
 can provide useful information from your plugin that might be helpful to operators and clients of Vault clusters. Any plugin running in Vault Enterprise 1.16+ can generate such events.

This article assumes that the plugin is written in the Go programming language. It is possible for non-Go plugins to send events as well by calling the [`SendEvent` gRPC call](https://github.com/hashicorp/vault/blob/da00addcb60e5fc4fef308715c0a1bd81c475167/sdk/plugin/pb/backend.proto#L713)
 directly.

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.18.x/plugins/plugin-development-event-notifications#sending-event-notifications-in-plugins)
Sending event notifications in plugins
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Sending event notifications within a plugin is as simple as calling:

    err := logical.SendEvent(ctx, b, eventType, metadata...)
    

where:

*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.18.x/plugins/plugin-development-event-notifications#)
    [`ctx`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.18.x/plugins/plugin-development-event-notifications#ctx)
     is a `context.Context` interface that is currently unused,
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.18.x/plugins/plugin-development-event-notifications#)
    [`b`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.18.x/plugins/plugin-development-event-notifications#b)
     is your plugin backend, which implements the `logical.EventSender` interface,
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.18.x/plugins/plugin-development-event-notifications#)
    [`eventType`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.18.x/plugins/plugin-development-event-notifications#eventtype)
     is a string for your event type,
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.18.x/plugins/plugin-development-event-notifications#)
    [`metadata`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.18.x/plugins/plugin-development-event-notifications#metadata)
     is a list of strings, which are interpreted as pairs that will be added to the event notification metadata, e.g., `metadata := []string{"key1", "value1", "key2", "value2"}` means that the metadata will include the pairs `key1: value1` and `key2: value2`, and,
*   [](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.18.x/plugins/plugin-development-event-notifications#)
    [`logical`](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.18.x/plugins/plugin-development-event-notifications#logical)
     refers to `github.com/hashicorp/vault/sdk/logical`.

`logical.SendEvent` is non-blocking and sends the event notifications asynchronously.

Note: It is possible to call `b.SendEvent()` directly, but it is less convenient since it requires a full protobuf struct to be passed in.

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.18.x/plugins/plugin-development-event-notifications#best-practices)
Best practices
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

While there are no strict rules for what events a plugin may generate, there are some best practices to follow:

*   **Do _not_ include sensitive or secret data** in the event notification. Events are not protected by the same policy as the underlying secret.
    
*   Only generate event notificiations on writes and actionable errors, **not** reads. Generally, reads are more frequent than writes so generating event notifications for every read operation could have a negative impact on the server performance. Operators that need to be notified when information is read are usually better served by using Vault [audit logs](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/audit)
    .
    
*   Prefix event types with your plugin name to avoid clashing with other plugins' event types. For example, the database plugin uses the `database/` prefix for all of its event notifications.
    
*   Use [common metadata keys](https://github.com/hashicorp/vault/blob/da00addcb60e5fc4fef308715c0a1bd81c475167/sdk/logical/events.go#L13-L25)
    , such as `data_path` for the API path to the secret the event notification is about, and `operation` to indicate what modification took place.
    
*   Document the event types that your plugin produces, including descriptions of when those are generated.
    
*   Use `logical.SendEvent()` to send event notifications. Although it is possible to call the `framework.Backend` or gRPC methods directly to send event notifications, `logical.SendEvent` abstracts away much of that for you.
    
*   Send event notifications at the end of your methods, or in a `defer`, to ensure that the event notification has the most up-to-date information.
    
*   Fail gracefully if the event notification system is unavailble. This is detected as an error when sending events, such as `framework.ErrNoEvents`, which usually occurs when running on older versions of Vault where event notifications are not present or have been disabled.
    
*   Generate events in all editions of Vault. Even though it is not possible to subscribe to event notifications in the Community Edition of Vault, plugins are generally unaware of what edition of Vault they are connected to and should generate events regardless.
    

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.18.x/plugins/plugin-development-event-notifications#examples)
Examples
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Here are some examples of adding event notifications to the KV and database plugins in Vault:

*   [https://github.com/hashicorp/vault-plugin-secrets-kv/pull/88](https://github.com/hashicorp/vault-plugin-secrets-kv/pull/88)
    
*   [https://github.com/hashicorp/vault/pull/24718](https://github.com/hashicorp/vault/pull/24718)
    

[Edit this page on GitHub](https://github.com/hashicorp/web-unified-docs/blob/main/content/vault/v1.18.x/content/docs/plugins/plugin-development-event-notifications.mdx)

---

# Create a lease count quota | Vault | HashiCorp Developer

[HashiConf The full conference agenda's now LIVE. Buy your pass and plan your schedule. Register](https://www.hashicorp.com/en/conferences/hashiconf/register)

Dismiss alert

[Vault Home](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault)

You are viewing documentation for version v1.17.x. [View latest version](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/configuration/create-lease-count-quota)
.

Create a lease count quota
==========================

Use lease count quotas to limit the number of leases generated on a per-mount basis and control resource consumption for your Vault instance where hard limits makes sense.

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.17.x/configuration/create-lease-count-quota#before-you-start)
Before you start
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   **Confirm you have access to the root or administration namespace for your Vault instance**. Modifying lease count quotas is a restricted activity.

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.17.x/configuration/create-lease-count-quota#step-1-determine-the-appropriate-granularity)
Step 1: Determine the appropriate granularity
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The granularity of your lease limits can affect the performance of your Vault cluster. In particular, if your lease limits cause the number of rejected requests to increase dramatically, the increased audit logging may impact Vault performance.

Review past system behavior to identify whether the quota limits should be inheritable or limited to a specific role.

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.17.x/configuration/create-lease-count-quota#step-2-apply-the-count-quota)
Step 2: Apply the count quota
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

CLIAPI

Use `vault write` and the `sys/quotas/lease-count/{quota-name}` mount path to create a new lease count quota:

    $ vault write                           \
        sys/quotas/lease-count/<QUOTA_NAME> \
        name="<QUOTA_NAME>"                 \
        path="<PLUGIN_MOUNT_PATH>"          \
        role="<OPTIONAL_AUTHN_ROLE>"        \
        max_leases=<LEASE_LIMIT>
    

For example, to create a targeted quota limit called **webapp-tokens** on the `webapp` role for the `approle` plugin at the default mount path:

    $ vault write                            \
        sys/quotas/lease-count/webapp-tokens \
        name="webapp-tokens"                 \
        path="auth/approle"                  \
        role="webapp"                        \
        max_leases=100
    
    Success! Data written to: sys/quotas/lease-count/webapp-tokens
    

1.  Create a payload file with your quota settings.
    
        {
          "name": "<QUOTA_NAME>",
          "path": "<PLUGIN_MOUNT_PATH>",
          "role": "<OPTIONAL_AUTHN_ROLE>",
          "max_leases": <LEASE_LIMIT>,
        }
        
    
    For example, to create a targeted quota limit called **webapp-tokens** on the `webapp` role for the `approle` plugin at the default mount path:
    
        {
          "name": "webapp-tokens",
          "path": "auth/approle",
          "role": "webapp",
          "max_leases": 100,
        }
        
    
2.  Call the `/sys/quotas/lease-count/{quota-name}` endpoint to apply the lease count quota. For example, to apply the `webapp-tokens` quota:
    
        $ curl \
         --request POST \
         --header "X-Vault-Token: ${VAULT_TOKEN}" \
         --data @payload.json \
         ${VAULT_ADDR}/v1/sys/quotas/lease-count/webapp-tokens
        
    

Silent endpoint

The `/sys/quotas/lease-count/{quota-name}` endpoint succeeds silently.

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.17.x/configuration/create-lease-count-quota#step-3-confirm-the-quota-settings)
Step 3: Confirm the quota settings
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

CLIAPI

Use `vault read` and the `sys/quotas/lease-count/{quota-name}` mount path to display the lease count quota details:

    $ vault read sys/quotas/lease-count/<QUOTA_NAME>
    

For example, to read the **webapp-tokens** quota details:

    $ vault read sys/quotas/lease-count/webapp-tokens
    
    Key            Value
    ---            -----
    counter        0
    inheritable    true
    max_leases     100
    name           webapp-tokens
    path           auth/approle/
    role           webapp
    type           lease-count
    

Call the `sys/quotas/lease-count/{quota-name}` endpoint to display the lease count quota details. For example, to read the **webapp-tokens** quota details:

    $ curl                                      \
      --header "X-Vault-Token: ${VAULT_TOKEN}"  \
      --request GET                             \
      --silent                                  \
      ${VAULT_ADDR}/v1/sys/quotas/lease-count/webapp-tokens | jq
    
    {
      "request_id": "188e22f1-dc1a-251a-a0a1-005e256fe70f",
      "lease_id": "",
      "renewable": false,
      "lease_duration": 0,
      "data": {
        "counter": 0,
        "inheritable": false,
        "max_leases": 100,
        "name": "webapp-tokens",
        "path": "auth/approle/",
        "role": "webapp",
        "type": "lease-count"
      },
      "wrap_info": null,
      "warnings": null,
      "auth": null
    }
    

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.17.x/configuration/create-lease-count-quota#next-steps)
Next steps
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Proactive monitoring and periodic usage analysis can help you identify potential problems before they escalate.

*   Brush up on [general Vault resource quotas](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/concepts/resource-quotas)
     in general.
*   Learn about [lease count quotas for Vault Enterprise](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/enterprise/lease-count-quotas)
    .
*   Learn how to [query audit device logs](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/tutorials/monitoring/query-audit-device-logs)
    .
*   Review [key Vault metrics for common health checks](https://developer.hashicorp.com/well-architected-framework/reliability/reliability-vault-monitoring-key-metrics)
    .

[Edit this page on GitHub](https://github.com/hashicorp/web-unified-docs/blob/main/content/vault/v1.17.x/content/docs/configuration/create-lease-count-quota.mdx)

---

# version - Command | Vault | HashiCorp Developer

[HashiConf The full conference agenda's now LIVE. Buy your pass and plan your schedule. Register](https://www.hashicorp.com/en/conferences/hashiconf/register)

Dismiss alert

[Vault Home](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault)

You are viewing documentation for version v1.16.x. [View latest version](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/commands/print)
.

print
=====

The `print` command prints the Vault token currently in use. The only available subcommand is `token`.

[](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/v1.16.x/commands/print#examples)
Examples
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Print the current token.

    $ vault print token
    hvs.CAESICaie3Dm0_Hx001QuMabo1IXnyKkx_FuE14MH7zir_bqGh4KHGh2cy5wQnJsZzZ6WG82b29HUlI3eFdEQ0NPQzQ
    

[Edit this page on GitHub](https://github.com/hashicorp/web-unified-docs/blob/main/content/vault/v1.16.x/content/docs/commands/print.mdx)
